---
title: "Data Wrangling"
subtitle: "Learn R with Dr. Hu and His Friends"
author: "Yue Hu (Department of Political Science, Tsinghua University)"

webr:
  packages:
    - drhur
    - dplyr
    - tidyr
---

{{< include _extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

# Data Wrangling {#sec-munging-en}

::: {.callout-note title="Chapter Highlights"}

1. Single-dataset operations
    - Explore
    - Wrangle
    - Filter
    - Modify
2. Cross-dataset operations
    - Direct merging
    - Index merging

*Research question*: Drawing on the WVS7 sample (`wvs7`), what useful information does it contain regarding the factors that shape people's views of equality?

:::

## Data Exploration

- Data exploration: initial inspection of an unfamiliar dataset — its composition, structure, format, and content
- A follow-up to data input, and the first step of any data analysis
- Simplest method: "just look at the data" — call the object name directly

```{webr}
#| label: glimpse

# Load the data
data("wvs7", package = "drhur")

# Inspect the data frame
wvs7
```

---

::: {.callout-tip title="The `tibble` data frame"}

- `wvs7` is stored as a `tibble` — an upgraded data frame
- It still has rows, columns, and cells
- Almost all functions for `data.frame` also work on `tibble`
- It is the standard format across the tidyverse toolchain

:::

---

- Direct inspection is convenient but breaks down when data are large
- We need more systematic ways to look at structure and variable characteristics

---

- Data structure = the size and composition of the dataset: number of observations, variables, variable names
- Probe functions usually have abbreviated English names
- Example: `str` = "structure", listing variable types, row count, column count

```{webr}
#| label: systemView

library(dplyr)

# Number of rows
nrow(wvs7)

# Number of columns
ncol(wvs7)

# Variable names (first 6 only)
head(names(wvs7))

# The tidyverse-preferred structure inspector
glimpse(wvs7)
```

---

- The data has 1264 rows and 24 columns
- `glimpse()` displays the data more compactly:
    - Column names listed vertically
    - Each variable type annotated (`<fct>` factor, `<dbl>` double, etc.)
    - Preview of the first few values

---

- Two ways to extract a variable:
    - Bracket form: `<df>[, "name"]`
    - Dollar-sign form: `<df>$<name>` (the more common)

```{webr}
#| label: variable_extract

# Bracket form (showing first 6 rows only)
head(wvs7[, "country"])

# Dollar-sign form (showing first 10 unique values only)
unique(wvs7$country) |> head(10)
```

---

- For numeric variables: `mean()`, `median()`, `max()`, `var()`, `summary()`, etc.

```{webr}
#| label: varCal_numeric

# Mean age of respondents
mean(wvs7$age, na.rm = TRUE)

# Income-level distribution summary
summary(wvs7$incomeLevel)
```

---

- For categorical and ordinal variables: `unique()` for the value set, `table()` for the frequency distribution

```{webr}
#| label: varCal_categorical

# Frequency distribution of age
table(wvs7$age)

# Frequency distribution of gender
table(wvs7$female)

# Frequency distribution of marital status
table(wvs7$marital)
```

---

- `table()` returns different output for different variable types — another instance of OOP polymorphism

## Data Wrangling with `dplyr`

- Data wrangling = purposefully picking variables, filtering observations, and reshaping data
- Base R has wrangling functions; `tidyverse` makes them more intuitive
- The two workhorses: `dplyr` (cleaning) plus parts of `tidyr`

---

- `tidyverse` is a meta-package (a collection of R packages)
- Members include `tidyr`, `dplyr`, `stringr`, `tibble`
- They share a common data structure and design philosophy (the "tidy universe")
- `library(tidyverse)` loads the core members at once

---

- `dplyr` specializes in data cleaning
- Goal: reshape raw data into an analysis-ready form
- Includes adjustments to variable names, types, value representations

---

- The pipe (`|>`): hands the result of one command to the next
- R's native pipe is `|>`; `dplyr` adds `%>%`
- Visualizes commands in execution order; reads naturally

---

- Traditional nested style (read inside-out, hard to follow):

```r
# Eating dumplings, nested
eatDumpling <- eat(
  dip(
    cook(
      fill(
        mix(meat, with = c(salt, soy_sauce, green_onion, ginger)),
        with = wrapper
      ),
      in_water = boiled_water
    ),
    in_sauce = vinegar
  )
)
```

---

- Pipe style (read top-down, logic is clear):

```r
# Eating dumplings, piped
eatDumpling_pipe <- mix(meat, with = c(salt, soy_sauce, green_onion, ginger)) |>
  fill(with = wrapper) |>
  cook(in_water = boiled_water) |>
  dip(in_sauce = vinegar) |>
  eat()
```

- Read `|>` as "then" or "and then" — function chains become close to natural language

---

::: {.callout-note title="Pipe shortcut"}

- Windows / Linux: `Ctrl + Shift + M`
- macOS: `Cmd + Shift + M`

:::

---

- Four common wrangling operations: select, filter, arrange, mutate

---

- Selecting variables = picking columns
- Use `select()`
- Syntax: `select(<df>, <var1>, <var2>, ...)`

```{webr}
#| label: select

library(dplyr)

# Pick five variables of interest
select(wvs7, equalIncentive, incomeLevel, confidence_gov, education, country)
```

---

- Pattern-based selection helpers:
    - `starts_with()`: starts with a given string
    - `ends_with()`: ends with a given string
    - `matches()`: regular-expression match anywhere

```{webr}
#| label: select_startwith

# All variables beginning with "corruption"
select(wvs7, starts_with("corruption"))
```

---

::: {.callout-tip title="Pattern-based helpers"}

- `starts_with`: opening characters
- `ends_with`: closing characters
- `matches`: regular-expression match (most powerful)
- Note: third-person singular `s` — easy to forget

:::

---

- Drop variables by prepending `-`
- `select(wvs7, -corruption_state)` drops one variable
- `select(wvs7, -(varA:varB))` drops a contiguous range
- Can combine with helpers like `starts_with()`

```{webr}
#| label: deselect

# Keep corruption_local and corruption_civil, drop corruption_state
select(wvs7, starts_with("corruption"), -corruption_state)
```

---

- `rename()` changes a variable name
- Syntax: `rename(<df>, <new_name> = <old_name>)`
- Multiple names can be changed at once

```{webr}
#| label: rename

# Rename 'country' to 'nationality'
rename(wvs7, nationality = country)
```

---

- Note: `rename()` only prints a preview — it does not overwrite the original data frame
- To make the change permanent, assign back with `<-`
- Example: `wvs7 <- rename(wvs7, nationality = country)`

---

- `filter()` keeps rows that satisfy a logical condition
- Syntax: `filter(<df>, <condition1>, <condition2>, ...)`
- Multiple conditions separated by commas — equivalent to logical AND

```{webr}
#| label: filter

# Single condition: only South Korea
select(wvs7, equalIncentive, country) |>
  filter(country == "South Korea")

# Set condition: South Korea OR Japan
select(wvs7, equalIncentive, country) |>
  filter(country %in% c("South Korea", "Japan"))
```

---

- `filter()` supports any logical or comparison expression
- Compound example: Japanese respondents over age 50

```{webr}
#| label: filter_compound

# Two conditions at once
select(wvs7, equalIncentive, country, age) |>
  filter(country == "Japan", age > 50)
```

---

::: {.callout-note title="Why didn't the rename stick?"}

- `rename()` does not automatically modify the data frame stored in your environment
- To make the change permanent, you must explicitly assign with `<-`
- Otherwise the original variable name remains in the next step

:::

---

- `arrange()` sorts rows by variable values
- Syntax: `arrange(<df>, <var1>, <var2>, ...)`
- Default order is ascending (smallest to largest)

```{webr}
#| label: arrange

# Sort by income level (low to high)
select(wvs7, equalIncentive, incomeLevel, country) |>
  filter(country == "Japan") |>
  arrange(incomeLevel)
```

---

- Two ways to do descending:
    - Wrap the variable in `desc()`
    - Prepend a minus sign `-` (numeric variables only)

```{webr}
#| label: arrange_desc

# Method 1: desc() helper
select(wvs7, equalIncentive, incomeLevel, country) |>
  filter(country == "Japan") |>
  arrange(desc(incomeLevel))

# Method 2: minus sign (numeric only)
select(wvs7, equalIncentive, incomeLevel, country) |>
  filter(country == "Japan") |>
  arrange(-incomeLevel)
```

---

- `arrange()` also supports multiple sort keys
- Order matters: ties on the first key are resolved by the second

```{webr}
#| label: arrange_compound

# Sort by income first; ties go to age
select(wvs7, equalIncentive, incomeLevel, age) |>
  arrange(incomeLevel, age)
```

---

::: {.callout-warning title="Order of sort keys matters"}

- Argument order in `select` and `filter` mostly does not matter
- In `arrange`, **argument order determines sort priority**
- Swapping the keys completely changes the result

:::

```{webr}
#| label: arrange_order

# Sort by age first; ties go to income
select(wvs7, equalIncentive, incomeLevel, age) |>
  arrange(age, incomeLevel)
```

---

- Data modification: reshape how a variable is encoded
- Example: collapse a 10-level income variable into a "poor / rich" binary
- Use `mutate()` — syntax: `mutate(<df>, <new_var> = <expression>)`

```{webr}
#| label: mutate

# New variable poor: TRUE if income <= 3
select(wvs7, equalIncentive, incomeLevel) |>
  mutate(poor = incomeLevel <= 3)
```

---

::: {.callout-important title="Modify, not invent"}

- The English word "mutate" means "transform"
- The function's spirit: not create from nothing, but reshape existing variables into new ones

:::

---

- `mutate()` can create several new variables at once, separated by `,`
- It can generate multiple new variables from the same source

```{webr}
#| label: mutate_multiple

# Create poor and rich in one call
select(wvs7, equalIncentive, incomeLevel) |>
  mutate(
    poor = incomeLevel <= 3,
    rich = incomeLevel >= 6
  )
```

---

::: {.callout-warning title="Avoid accidental overwrites"}

- `mutate` allows you to use the same name twice — the later assignment overwrites the earlier
- In a single pipeline, assigning the same name to two different expressions discards the first

:::

```{webr}
#| label: mutate_overwrite

# poor is defined twice; the second wins
select(wvs7, country, incomeLevel) |>
  mutate(
    poor = incomeLevel <= 3,
    poor = incomeLevel <= 5
  )
```

## Merging Datasets

- Data merging: combine multiple data frames into a single new one
- Single-source data carry limited information; multi-source merging amplifies analytical value
- Two paths: direct merging, and index merging

---

- Direct merging: stack same-structured data vertically or horizontally
- Two flavors:
    - Row-binding: equal column count required; `bind_rows()`
    - Column-binding: equal row count required; `bind_cols()`

```{webr}
#| label: bindRow

# Mock data 1: first 3 South-Korean rows
wvs7_sk3 <- filter(wvs7, country == "South Korea") |>
  head(n = 3)

# Mock data 2: first 3 Japanese rows
wvs7_ja3 <- filter(wvs7, country == "Japan") |>
  head(n = 3)

# Row-bind
bind_rows(wvs7_sk3, wvs7_ja3)
```

---

::: {.callout-warning title="Unequal-column row-binding"}

- `bind_rows` still works when columns do not match
- Mismatched cells are filled with `NA`
- Useful when source structures differ slightly

:::

```{webr}
#| label: bindRow_unequal

# Mock data 1: income and country only
wvs7_sk3_income <- select(wvs7, incomeLevel, country) |>
  filter(country == "South Korea") |>
  head(n = 3)

# Mock data 2: country and government confidence only
wvs7_ja3_confidence <- select(wvs7, country, confidence_gov) |>
  filter(country == "Japan") |>
  head(n = 3)

# Unequal-column row-bind: mismatched cells become NA
bind_rows(wvs7_sk3_income, wvs7_ja3_confidence)
```

---

- Column-binding: use `bind_cols`
- The function name is plural — do not forget the "s"

```{webr}
#| label: bindCols

# Prep 1: all confidence variables
wvs7_confidence <- select(wvs7, starts_with("confidence"))

# Prep 2: country variable
wvs7_country <- select(wvs7, country)

# Column-bind
bind_cols(wvs7_confidence, wvs7_country)
```

---

- Index merging: combine two datasets on shared index values
- Safer than direct merging: as long as index variables agree in structure, the merge is correct
- Supports one-to-one and one-to-many

---

::: {.callout-important title="Structural consistency"}

- Structural consistency = the index variables follow the same coding scheme
- The variables need not cover every possible value
- Example: with "gender" as index (0=male, 1=female), a dataset containing only women can still be merged correctly if the scheme is preserved

:::

---

- Demonstration datasets:
    - `wvs7_eq`: individual-level data, Japan + South Korea
    - `country_info`: country-level data, Japan + Thailand
- Both have country-name variables that serve as the index, though under different names

```{webr}
#| label: join_data

# Data 1: individual-level data for Japan and South Korea
wvs7_eq <- select(wvs7, country, equalIncentive, female, education) |>
  filter(country %in% c("Japan", "South Korea"))

# Data 2: country-level data for Japan and Thailand
country_info <- filter(wvs7, country %in% c("Japan", "Thailand")) |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarise(across(female:education, \(x) mean(x, na.rm = TRUE))) |>
  ungroup() |>
  rename(nation = country)

# Inspect
wvs7_eq
country_info
```

---

- `dplyr` provides four join types:
    - **Left join `left_join`**: keep all rows of the left table; merge in the right where keys match; right-only keys are dropped
    - **Right join `right_join`**: mirror of the left join
    - **Inner join `inner_join`**: keep only rows whose key appears in both (intersection)
    - **Full join `full_join`**: keep all keys from either side (union); unmatched cells become `NA`
- Generic syntax: `<type>_join(<data1>, <data2>, by = join_by(<index>))`

```{webr}
#| label: join

# Run all four join types
wvs7_lj <- left_join(wvs7_eq, country_info, by = join_by(country == nation))
wvs7_rj <- right_join(wvs7_eq, country_info, by = join_by(country == nation))
wvs7_ij <- inner_join(wvs7_eq, country_info, by = join_by(country == nation))
wvs7_fj <- full_join(wvs7_eq, country_info, by = join_by(country == nation))

# Compare which countries each retained
wvs7_lj$country |> unique()
wvs7_rj$country |> unique()
wvs7_ij$country |> unique()
wvs7_fj$country |> unique()
```

## Section Exercise {.unnumbered}

**Exercise 1**: A comprehensive `dplyr` pipeline

Build a single `dplyr` pipeline on `wvs7` that:

1. Creates a new variable `avg_confidence` — the arithmetic mean of `confidence_gov` and `confidence_court`
2. Keeps respondents who satisfy:
    - `country` equals `"Japan"`
    - `age` strictly between 40 and 60
    - `avg_confidence` greater than 3
3. Sorts the result by `avg_confidence` in descending order
4. Returns only `age`, `incomeLevel`, `equalIncentive`, `avg_confidence`

::: { .hint exercise="dplyr_pipeline" }

- Use `mutate()` to add the new variable
- Use `filter()` for the conditions
- Use `arrange(desc(...))` for descending order
- Use `select()` to keep only the target columns
- Order: mutate → filter → arrange → select

:::

```{webr}
#| label: dplyr_pipeline
#| exercise: dplyr_pipeline

library(dplyr)
data("wvs7", package = "drhur")

# Build the pipeline here

```

::: { .solution exercise="dplyr_pipeline" }

```r
library(dplyr)
data("wvs7", package = "drhur")

wvs7 |>
  mutate(avg_confidence = (confidence_gov + confidence_court) / 2) |>
  filter(
    country == "Japan",
    age > 40,
    age < 60,
    avg_confidence > 3
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(avg_confidence)) |>
  select(age, incomeLevel, equalIncentive, avg_confidence)

# Walk-through:
# 1. mutate: new avg_confidence as mean of government and court confidence
# 2. filter: Japanese respondents, age strictly within (40, 60), avg_confidence > 3
# 3. arrange(desc(...)): descending order by avg_confidence
# 4. select: keep only the four target columns
```

:::

## Chapter Recap

::: {.callout-note title="A friendly reminder"}

This chapter and the chapters that follow will cover quite a few concepts.
Some of them you do not need to master at first sight — a general impression is enough, and many will deepen as you encounter them again in later chapters.
Others, however, form the core foundation of this stage. Missing them now will hinder your understanding later.
Each chapter therefore ends with a key-point recap that flags what you must hold onto at this moment.

:::

---

- Data exploration
    - Row / column count: `nrow()` / `ncol()`
    - Structure: `glimpse()`
    - Variable names: `names()`
    - Unique values: `unique()`
    - Frequency distribution: `table()`
- Data wrangling
    - Pipe: `|>`
    - Select columns: `select()`
    - Rename: `rename()`
    - Filter rows: `filter()`
    - Arrange: `arrange()`
    - Modify / add: `mutate()`
- Data merging
    - Row-bind: `bind_rows()`
    - Column-bind: `bind_cols()`
    - Left join: `left_join()`
    - Right join: `right_join()`
    - Inner join: `inner_join()`
    - Full join: `full_join()`
